cards.front: What are the five main debugging techniques?
cards.back:
• Code inspection
  • Running the program
  • Print statements
  • Logging
  • Using a debugger

cards.text: {{c1::Code inspection}} involves examining the code directly to find errors, and works best for {{c2::small programs}} or when you have a good idea where the problem is.

cards.front: Why is running the program an important debugging technique?
cards.back: It helps you observe the program's behavior, see what inputs cause problems, and reproduce the error consistently.

cards.text: Using {{c1::print statements}} (like std::cout) to output variable values at different points helps you track program {{c2::state}} and find where values become incorrect.

cards.front: What is the advantage of logging over print statements?
cards.back: Logging can be turned on/off conditionally, provides timestamps, severity levels, and can output to files rather than cluttering console output.

cards.front: What is the main advantage of using an integrated debugger over print statements?
cards.back: A debugger lets you pause execution, inspect variables, step through code line-by-line, and examine the call stack without modifying source code.

cards.concept: When to use different debugging techniques
cards.explanation:
• Code inspection: Small programs, obvious errors
  • Print statements: Quick checks, simple issues
  • Logging: Complex programs needing persistent debugging info
  • Debugger: Complex bugs, need to inspect program state in detail

cards.front: What should you do if a debugging technique isn't working?
cards.back: Try a different technique. If one approach isn't helping you find the error, switch to another debugging method.

cards.text: Being able to {{c1::reproduce}} a bug consistently is important because it allows you to test whether your {{c2::fix}} actually works.

cards.front: Why might print statements be problematic for debugging?
cards.back: They clutter your code, must be removed later, can change program behavior (especially timing), and are hard to turn off/on conditionally.

cards.text: The vast majority of debugging time is spent {{c1::finding where the error is}}, not fixing it.

cards.concept: Best practices for finding bugs faster
cards.explanation:
• Write code incrementally and test often
  • Reproduce the bug consistently
  • Use the appropriate debugging technique
  • Narrow down the problem location systematically
  • Understand the cause before attempting a fix
